next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

Joel sat in the Adirondack chair that had been Cliff's two nights ago and watched a BCA tech in nitrile gloves walk the stone path from the dock toward the house with an evidence bag held away from his leg. From this distance, through pine and birch, the tech looked like a man cleaning up after a picnic. Careful. Methodical. The kind of man who didn't miss what other people wished he'd miss.

Tuesday afternoon. September light came low through the trees and threw gold across the lake. The water was flat. A motorboat hummed somewhere past the far shore. The loons hadn't started yet.

He hadn't slept. All night the house had kept making Martin noises: the refrigerator clicking on, the third stair complaining under somebody's weight at three in the morning, the hot tub pump running its maintenance cycle for nobody. Joel had lain in the guest bed listening and setting one fact against the next until a pattern started to hold. Except there was nowhere clean to put it. Just a house where two men had died in four days and five others were still moving through the rooms like nobody had told the furniture what happened.

Paul came out through the slider with two mugs. He'd made coffee four times since dawn. Joel had quit trying to decide what that meant. Paul set one mug on the chair arm and sat down, not in Martin's chair. Nobody had sat in Martin's chair since Saturday morning.

"She talked to Nate again," Paul said. "Forty minutes, maybe a little more."

"About what."

"The audio. Cliff's phone. Monday night."

Joel took the mug. Too hot. Too weak. Paul had been mak...
open_hooks_to_carry: Mallick returns: no casserole, no wrong names — she gets Martin's name right and the guests notice
The house locked down as a crime scene, forensics called
Mallick examines Cliff's body: head wound inconsistent with a fall, water in lungs indicating submersion, wrong shoes
Mallick applies her correction map from the Martin investigation to Cliff's death — who had access, motive, opportunity
Her private assessment: someone strong, desperate, and careless enough to get the shoes wrong
The contrast between this Mallick and casserole Mallick should be stark and earned

payoffs_to_land.description: The correction map Mallick built during the comic investigation — who knew which rooms, who corrected which details, who was where — now becomes the operational tool for solving Cliff's murder.

Nate's podcast recordings from draft night and subsequent evenings contain ambient audio — footsteps, voices, timing — that helps Mallick build a timeline. The recordings Nate made for content become evidence.

prev_tail_excerpt:
...'s soaked khakis and Doug's wet right hand. "I told her we already touched things."

"I was trying to help him," Paul said, and his voice cracked on the last word. Not a sob. Just the sound of a measured man's measurement giving way for a second.

"I know," Joel said.

Nate sat down on the dock next to Doug. Not close, three feet between them, but on the dock because Doug was on the dock and that was the point. Nate's bare feet dangled over the edge, inches above the water where Cliff was floating. He realized it, muttered "shit," and pulled them back under him.

The wait settled over them in separate pieces. Paul kept the flashlight on the planks. Joel stood at the path, listening for tires. Tommy stayed where he'd stopped, too still, his hands opening and closing at his sides only when he thought nobody was looking. Doug kept seeing Cliff as he'd been in the great room two hours earlier, dry loafers on hardwood, Martin's scotch in his hand, talking about taxes and process like the future still existed.

Headlights appeared at the far end of the gravel driveway, distant at first, then the crunch of tires reached the house. Joel straightened. Paul's flashlight dropped to his side. Nate stayed seated for another second before pushing himself up.

Doug stood. His sweatpants were soaked, his knees ached from the dock, and his right hand still smelled like lake water and Cliff's skin. One last look at the water: hair drifting in the moonlight, loafers dark below the surface, the gash a black line. Then Doug turned away and walked up the dock toward the house and the headlights.
secondary_character_beats: Each surviving guest's reaction to the new Mallick should be distinct — Joel recognizes the real detective, Tommy recognizes the danger, Nate can't stop talking, Doug goes silent, Paul tries to help
state_deltas_required: a real homicide with real suspects — Mallick without the folksy persona
Mallick is investigating a real murder; the corrections from the comedy become the evidentiary map for the tragedy
